title: Acute Coronary Syndrome — STEMI
oneLiner: Occlusive coronary thrombosis causing transmural myocardial necrosis; presents with crushing chest pain, ST-elevation on ECG, and high mortality if not reperfused within 120 minutes.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
You are bleeped to the ED resus bay. A 58-year-old man with a 30-pack-year smoking history, known hypertension, presents with a 90-minute history of central, “elephant-on-chest” chest pain radiating to his left arm and jaw. He is pale, sweaty, and clammy. He tells you he feels “impending doom.” His heart rate is 110 bpm, BP 145/90, respiratory rate 22, oxygen saturations 94% on room air. He has had one episode of vomiting.

**Pattern Clues Dissected:**
- **Central crushing pain radiating to left arm/jaw** → classic myocardial ischaemia; referred pain via shared spinal afferents (C8-T4)
- **Pale, sweaty, clammy (diaphoresis)** → sympathetic activation from pain, anxiety, and reduced cardiac output; endogenous catecholamine surge
- **Impending doom** → autonomic storm; often a prodrome of acute MI
- **Tachycardia** → compensatory response (or pain/stress), but can be due to reduced LV function
- **Vomiting** → vagal stimulation (inferior wall MI more commonly causes nausea/vomiting due to Bezold-Jarisch reflex)
- **Smoking, hypertension** → established coronary artery disease risk factors

**Atypical Presentations (High-Yield Traps):**
1. **Silent MI in diabetics** – No chest pain. Presents with dyspnoea, fatigue, or hypotension. Autonomic neuropathy blunts afferent pain signals.
2. **Elderly patient with acute confusion** – Myocardial ischaemia can present as delirium, especially in those with baseline cognitive impairment. No chest pain complaint.
3. **Woman with epigastric pain/nausea** – Often misdiagnosed as dyspepsia. Females more likely to have non-obstructive CAD (MINOCA) but still can have ST-elevation.

---
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
STEMI = ST-Elevation Myocardial Infarction. It is the most severe form of acute coronary syndrome, defined by complete thrombotic occlusion of a coronary artery causing transmural ischaemia and necrosis of the myocardium supplied by that artery. The diagnosis requires both elevated cardiac biomarkers (troponin) and characteristic ECG changes (ST-elevation in two contiguous leads, or new LBBB).

**Why it matters:**
- **Frequency:** ~100,000 STEMIs per year in the UK. Leading cause of death globally.
- **Stakes:** Time = muscle. Every 30-minute delay to reperfusion increases 1-year mortality by ~7.5%. Early recognition and PCI/thrombolysis saves lives.
- **Common pitfalls:** Missing atypical presentations (especially in women/diabetics); failing to repeat ECG after 10 minutes if initial is non-diagnostic; delaying senior involvement.

---
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The story of a STEMI:**

It begins with **vulnerable plaque rupture** or erosion in a coronary artery. The plaque has a thin fibrous cap, a large lipid-rich core, and inflammatory cells (macrophages, T-lymphocytes). When the cap ruptures, subendothelial tissue factor is exposed to blood, triggering the **coagulation cascade**. Platelets adhere, activate, and aggregate. The fibrin mesh forms a **white clot** that grows into an **occlusive red thrombus** (fibrin-rich). This complete occlusion halts blood flow distal to the lesion.

- **Within 20 minutes:** Myocardial cells switch to anaerobic metabolism. ATP depleted. Sodium-potassium pump fails. Cell swelling.
- **Within 1-2 hours:** Irreversible necrosis begins. Necrosis wavefront travels from subendocardium to epicardium (transmural progression). This is why reperfusion within 120 minutes is the golden window.

**Mechanism -> Key Clinical Signs:**

| Pathophysiological Step | Clinical Manifestation |
|------------------------|------------------------|
| Complete coronary occlusion | ST-elevation on ECG (due to transmural ischaemia causing current of injury); reciprocal ST-depression in opposite leads |
| Myocyte necrosis | Troponin release (cTnI/cTnT); also CK-MB (less specific, faster rise) |
| Reduced LV systolic function | Hypotension, pulmonary oedema, cardiogenic shock if extensive |
| Sympathetic activation | Diaphoresis, pallor, tachycardia |
| Vagal stimulation (especially inferior MI, right coronary artery) | Nausea, vomiting, bradycardia, hypotension (Bezold-Jarisch reflex) |
| Ischaemic pain | Central chest pain radiating to arms/jaw due to referred pain via cardiac afferents (C8-T4) |
| Electrical instability | Ventricular arrhythmias (VT, VF) due to ischaemic myocardium acting as focus |

**Connect back to pattern recognition:** The patient's diaphoresis and tachycardia = sympathetic surge + reduced stroke volume. The vomiting = vagal activation from inferior ischaemia (often RCA occlusion). The ST-elevation on ECG = transmural current of injury.

---
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
**Most common cause:** Atherosclerotic plaque rupture (>95% of STEMIs).

| Category | Specific Cause | Stem Clue to Suspect |
|----------|---------------|----------------------|
| **Atherosclerotic** | Spontaneous plaque rupture with occlusive thrombus | Classic risk factors: smoking, HTN, DM, dyslipidaemia, age >45 men, >55 women |
| **Embolic** | Coronary artery embolism (e.g., from atrial fibrillation, infective endocarditis, prosthetic valve) | Sudden onset, no prodromal angina, known AF or recent valve surgery; may affect multiple territories |
| **Spontaneous coronary artery dissection (SCAD)** | Intimal tear leading to intramural haematoma (no atherosclerosis) | Young female, peripartum, fibromuscular dysplasia, intense physical/emotional stress |
| **Coronary vasospasm (variant/Prinzmetal angina)** | Focal epicardial coronary spasm (may occur at rest, often nocturnal) | Transient ST-elevation (often in sleep), associated with smoking/cocaine/methamphetamine; responds to vasodilators |
| **Thrombophilic states** | Hypercoagulable conditions (e.g., antiphospholipid syndrome, factor V Leiden, thrombocytosis) | History of prior unprovoked VTE; positive lupus anticoagulant; young age without traditional risk factors |
| **Cocaine/amphetamine use** | Vasospasm + increased O2 demand + prothrombotic state | Young patient, acute presentation, history of recreational drug use; often with agitation, tachycardia, hypertension |
| **Myocardial infarction with non-obstructive coronary arteries (MINOCA)** | No culprit stenosis >50%: can be due to coronary spasm, microvascular dysfunction, embolism, or myocarditis mimic | Females more common; angiography shows mild/no disease; requires further evaluation (MRI, OCT) |

**Key point:** Always consider SCAD in young women (especially peripartum) – do not give standard anti-thrombotic therapy blindly; discuss with cardiology.

---
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria (Fourth Universal Definition of MI, 2018):**  
- Rise/fall of cardiac troponin with at least one value >99th percentile upper reference limit, **AND**  
- At least one of:  
  - Symptoms of myocardial ischaemia  
  - New ischaemic ECG changes (ST-T changes, LBBB)  
  - Development of pathological Q waves  
  - Imaging evidence of new loss of viable myocardium or regional wall motion abnormality  
  - Identification of coronary thrombus on angiography  

**For STEMI specifically:** ST-elevation ≥1 mm in two contiguous limb leads (or ≥2 mm in two contiguous chest leads) **plus** positive troponin. But **do not wait for troponin** before activating the cath lab – ECG is the gatekeeper.

| Test | What It Shows | Why Order It | How to Interpret | Pattern Recognition |
|------|---------------|--------------|------------------|---------------------|
| **12-lead ECG** | ST-elevation, reciprocal depression, Q waves, new LBBB | Diagnostic for STEMI; localises culprit artery; identifies high-risk features (e.g., anterior ST-elevation, widespread hypotension) | *Isolated finding that confirms STEMI:* ST-elevation ≥1mm in contiguous leads (except V2-V3: ≥2mm in men ≥40y, ≥1.5mm in women) | Anterior leads V1-V4: LAD occlusion. Inferior leads II, III, aVF: RCA (often with reciprocal in aVL) or LCx. Posterior involvement: ST-depression V1-V3 ± tall R wave. |
| **High-sensitivity troponin (hs-cTnI or hs-cTnT)** | Cardiac myocyte necrosis markers | Confirms myocardial injury; prognostic value; guides timing of repeat measurement | **Single elevated value >99th percentile + dynamic change (rise/fall) = acute MI.** In STEMI, often very high (>10x ULN). | In STEMI, troponin may be normal initially if very early (<2h from pain onset); repeat at 3-6h. A single normal doesn't rule out. |
| **Chest X-ray** | Heart size, pulmonary oedema, aortic contour | Rule out other causes (dissection, pneumothorax); assess for heart failure | Cardiomegaly suggests chronic HTN; pulmonary oedema = acute LV failure; widened mediastinum → consider aortic dissection | Do not delay reperfusion for CXR if STEMI is clear |
| **Echocardiogram** | Regional wall motion abnormalities, LVEF, mechanical complications | Assess extent of ischaemia; detect complications (VSD, papillary muscle rupture, pericardial effusion) | New wall motion abnormality supports diagnosis; LVEF <40% indicates high risk; pericardial effusion ± tamponade suggests complications | Not essential for diagnosis in STEMI if clear ECG – may be done post-PCI |
| **Bloods (FBC, U&E, glucose, lipids)** | Baseline kidney function, electrolytes, anaemia, infection | Guided therapy (renal function for anti-thrombotics); correct hypokalaemia/hypomagnesaemia to reduce arrhythmia risk | Potassium <4.0 → high risk of VT/VF. eGFR <30 → cautious with LMWH/GPIIb/IIIa. | Always check glucose: stress hyperglycaemia worsens outcomes |

**What single finding confirms / rules out?**  
- **Confirms STEMI:** New ST-elevation in ≥2 contiguous leads + rise/fall in troponin.  
- **Rules out STEMI:** Normal ECG (no ST-elevation) and normal troponin (though unstable angina/NSTEMI possible – different topic).

---
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate — First Hour (Door-to-Balloon / Door-to-Needle target: ≤120 minutes from diagnosis)**

1. **MONA** (no longer standard – only Oxygen if hypoxic)  
   - **Morphine** 2-5 mg IV (titrate) → reduces pain, anxiety, and preload; caution: respiratory depression, nausea.  
   - **Oxygen** if SpO2 <90% → improves oxygenation; **do not give blindly** – hyperoxia can cause vasoconstriction and worsen injury.  
   - **Nitrates** GTN 400mcg sublingual (SL) every 5 min up to 3 doses → vasodilates coronary arteries and reduces preload; caution in RV infarct/hypotension.  
   - **Aspirin** 300 mg PO (chewed) → immediate platelet cyclooxygenase inhibition; reduces mortality by 23%.  

2. **Dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT)**  
   - **Prasugrel** 60 mg PO loading dose once (preferred over clopidogrel in STEMI) → faster, more potent P2Y12 inhibition.  
   - *Or* **Ticagrelor** 180 mg PO loading dose if prasugrel contraindicated (e.g., prior stroke/TIA, weight <60kg, age >75 for prasugrel caution).  
   - *Or* **Clopidogrel** 300-600 mg PO if above unavailable (less potent, slower onset).  

3. **Anticoagulation**  
   - **Unfractionated heparin (UFH):** 70-100 IU/kg IV bolus (max 5000 IU) → bolus then infusion (target APTT 50-70s). Used pre-PCI.  
   - *Or* **Enoxaparin** 0.5 mg/kg IV (then SC BID) – increasingly preferred in some protocols; check eGFR.  

4. **Reperfusion strategy**  
   - **Primary PCI (pPCI)** – if within 12 hours of symptom onset AND door-to-balloon <120 min AND PCI-capable hospital. Gold standard.  
     - Procedure: balloon angioplasty of culprit lesion + stent.  
     - While waiting: give UFH/enoxaparin, DAPT as above.  
   - **Thrombolysis** (e.g., alteplase, tenecteplase) – if pPCI not available within 120 min.  
     - Give within 30 min of diagnosis (door-to-needle).  
     - Contraindications: recent stroke, active bleeding, severe hypertension, aortic dissection.  
   - **Rescue PCI** – if thrombolysis fails (persistent ST-elevation >50% at 60-90 min, persistent pain).  

5. **Beta-blocker**  
   - **Bisoprolol (or metoprolol) PO** (e.g., bisoprolol 2.5-5 mg od) – reduce myocardial O2 demand, prevent arrhythmias. Contraindicated if acute heart failure, bradycardia, hypotension, cardiogenic shock.  

6. **High-dose statin**  
   - **Atorvastatin 80 mg PO** once daily – plaque stabilisation, lipid lowering, pleiotropic effects (anti-inflammatory). Start ASAP regardless of baseline LDL.  

**What NOT to do (common pitfalls):**  
- Do not give IV beta-blocker in acute phase (risk of cardiogenic shock) – use oral only.  
- Do not give nitrates if RV infarct (hypotension).  
- Do not delay PCI to wait for troponin result.  
- Do not give thrombolysis if patient has had a stroke in last 6 months or active bleeding.  

**Ward Management — 24-72 hours**

- Continue **DAPT** (aspirin 75 mg od lifelong + prasugrel 10 mg od / ticagrelor 90 mg bd for 12 months).  
- **Anticoagulation** (e.g., enoxaparin SC BID or UFH infusion) for at least 48 hours post-thrombolysis or until PCI.  
- **Beta-blocker** titrated to resting HR 50-60 bpm.  
- **ACE inhibitor** (e.g., ramipril 2.5-5 mg od) – start within 24h if BP permits, especially if LVEF <40%, anterior MI, or heart failure. Reduces remodelling and mortality.  
- **Mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist** (e.g., spironolactone 12.5-25 mg od) if LVEF ≤40% and symptomatic heart failure or diabetes (eplerenone if gynaecomastia).  
- **Monitor for complications** – arrhythmias, heart failure, bleeding, reinfarction.  
- **Echocardiogram** to assess LV function before discharge.  

**Long-Term Management**

- **Lifestyle:** smoking cessation, Mediterranean diet, exercise (cardiac rehabilitation), weight loss.  
- **Medical therapy:**  
  - Aspirin 75 mg od lifelong  
  - P2Y12 inhibitor for 12 months (prasugrel/ticagrelor) then switch to clopidogrel or aspirin alone  
  - High-dose statin (atorvastatin 80 mg od) lifelong  
  - Beta-blocker (e.g., bisoprolol 5-10 mg od) lifelong if MI within 3 years or LV dysfunction  
  - ACE inhibitor (e.g., ramipril 5-10 mg od) lifelong if LVEF <40% or hypertension  
- **Dual pathway inhibition** (rivaroxaban 2.5 mg bd + aspirin) if high-risk (e.g., polyvascular disease) and low bleeding risk – discuss with cardiology.  
- **Implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD)** if LVEF ≤35% despite ≥40 days of optimal medical therapy.  

---
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | STEMI | NSTEMI | Aortic Dissection | Acute Pericarditis |
|---------|-------|--------|-------------------|--------------------|
| **Pain** | Central crushing, radiating to arm/jaw, ± nausea, lasts >30 min | Similar but may be less severe, intermittent, or atypical | Sudden, severe, tearing anterior chest pain radiating to back, interscapular; may migrate | Sharp, pleuritic pain, worse lying flat, better leaning forward; often positional |
| **Exam** | Diaphoresis, pallor, ± hypotension, ± signs of heart failure | Similar but often less dramatic | Pulse deficit, BP difference >20 mmHg between arms, aortic regurgitation murmur, neurological deficits | Pericardial rub (triphasic), ± low-grade fever |
| **ECG** | ST-elevation in contiguous leads, reciprocal depression, new Q waves | ST-depression ± T wave inversion; no sustained ST-elevation (transient possible) | No specific pattern; may have ST-elevation if coronary ostia involved (rare); LVH strain pattern may mimic | Diffuse ST-elevation (concave upward) without reciprocal depression; PR depression |
| **Labs** | Troponin elevated (high), dynamic change; CK-MB may be high | Troponin elevated (any) but may be lower | Troponin normal or mildly elevated if coronary involved; D-dimer very high (rule out, not rule in) | Troponin normal or mildly elevated (<2x ULN); inflammatory markers (CRP, ESR) high |
| **Key diff** | Coronary angiography shows occlusive thrombus | Angiography shows non-occlusive stenosis or plaque ulceration | CT aortogram with contrast shows intimal flap and false lumen | Echocardiogram shows pericardial effusion, no wall motion abnormality; clinical response to NSAIDs |

---
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
| Complication | Presentation | Key Signs | What to Do |
|--------------|--------------|-----------|------------|
| **Cardiogenic shock** | Persistent hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg) despite fluids, end-organ hypoperfusion, altered mental status, oliguria | Cold clammy skin, tachycardia, narrow pulse pressure, raised JVP, pulmonary oedema | Call senior, ICU, cardiac catheterisation; urgent revascularisation (IABP/impella supportive); do not give beta-blockers |
| **Ventricular tachyarrhythmia (VT/VF)** | Sudden collapse, pulseless, cardiac arrest | ECG: VT/VF | Defibrillate (200J biphasic), CPR, amiodarone 300 mg IV after 3 shocks; treat underlying ischaemia |
| **Acute LV failure / pulmonary oedema** | Severe dyspnoea, orthopnoea, pink frothy sputum | Crackles throughout lung fields, raised JVP, SpO2 ↓ | Sit patient up, O2, furosemide 40-80 mg IV, nitrates if BP allows, CPAP, consider urgent PCI |
| **Right ventricular infarction** | Hypotension, clear lungs, raised JVP, Kussmaul's sign (JVP rises on inspiration) | ST-elevation in V4R (right-sided ECG), inferior MI | Fluid bolus (500-1000 mL crystalloid), do NOT give nitrates or diuretics; urgent revascularisation |
| **Mechanical complications** (free wall rupture, VSD, papillary muscle rupture) | Sudden severe hypotension, refractory heart failure, new pansystolic murmur (VSD/mitral regurgitation), syncope | ~3-7 days post-MI. Free wall rupture: electromechanical dissociation → cardiac arrest. VSD: harsh murmur at LLSB. Papillary muscle: acute mitral regurgitation, pulmonary oedema | Emergency echo, call cardiothoracic surgery; treat underlying (surgery/transcatheter repair) |
| **Pericarditis / Dressler syndrome** | Pleuritic chest pain, fever, pericardial rub 2-6 weeks post MI | Diffuse ST-elevation, PR depression, pericardial effusion on echo | NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400-800 mg TID) + colchicine; avoid steroids if possible (increase rupture risk) |
| **Late-presentation patterns** (untreated) | If missed and no reperfusion: patient presents with established Q waves on ECG, LV dysfunction, chronic heart failure, ventricular aneurysm, mural thrombus ± embolism | Q waves, low voltage, dyskinetic segment on echo | Anticoagulation if LV thrombus; ACEi, beta-blocker, diuretic for heart failure; ICD if LVEF ≤35% |

---
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

1. **STEMI Differential — "ABCDE"**  
   - A – Aortic dissection  
   - B – Boerhaave syndrome (oesophageal rupture) – pain + vomiting  
   - C – Cardiac (MI, pericarditis)  
   - D – Diaphragmatic (pneumothorax, PE)  
   - E – Epigastric (pancreatitis, biliary colic)  

2. **Indications for Thrombolysis in STEMI — "TIMI"** (not a strong mnemonic, but recall: time <12h, ST-elevation >1mm, new LBBB, ongoing pain) – but better:  
   **"Time Is Muscle"** – symptom onset <12h; contraindications: **"I BLEED"**  
   - I – Intracranial bleed ever  
   - B – Bleeding disorder / active bleed  
   - L – Liver disease severe  
   - E – Eye surgery (<6 weeks)  
   - E – Epilepsy (stroke mimic? ensure not SAH)  
   - D – Dissection (aortic)  

3. **ECG localisation of culprit artery — "LAD RCA LCx"**  
   - **LAD** (anterior/septal leads V1-4): anterior MI, worse prognosis  
   - **RCA** (inferior leads II, III, aVF; ± RV leads V4R): often bradycardia, hypotension (Bezold-Jarisch)  
   - **LCx** (lateral leads I, aVL, V5-6): often smaller area; can cause posterior MI (ST-depression V1-3)

**5-7 Clinical Pearls (Pattern Recognition Cues):**

1. **"Reciprocal ST-depression is the hallmark of STEMI"** – if you see ST-elevation in inferior leads, check aVL for reciprocal depression (rules out pericarditis).  
2. **"New LBBB + pain = STEMI until proven otherwise"** – activate cath lab immediately. Consider Sgarbossa criteria for diagnosis.  
3. **"In MI, troponin rises, but ECG is the gatekeeper"** – don't wait for troponin to make the call.  
4. **"Inferior MI with hypotension + clear lungs = RV infarct"** – give fluids, not nitrates.  
5. **"Posterior MI mimics anterior ST-depression"** – look for tall R waves in V1-3 and ST-elevation in posterior leads (V7-V9).  
6. **"Women and diabetics present atypically (dyspnoea, fatigue)"** – always do an ECG in at-risk patients with unexplained dyspnoea.  
7. **"Time from symptom onset to reperfusion is the single strongest predictor of outcome"** – target <120 min for PCI, <30 min for thrombolysis.

**Common Mistakes:**

- **Giving oxygen when SpO2 >95%** – harms (hyperoxia worsens ischaemia).  
- **Delaying PCI to wait for troponin** – ECG is sufficient.  
- **Giving beta-blocker in acute heart failure or cardiogenic shock** – can precipitate death.  
- **Assuming normal troponin + pain = no MI** – unstable angina exists (troponin negative).  
- **Forgetting to give a statin** – reduces recurrent events and mortality.

---
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
| Condition | When to Call | Who to Call | Specific Thresholds |
|-----------|--------------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Cardiogenic shock** | Immediately | Senior (Reg/Cons), ICU, Cardiac Catheter Lab | SBP <90 mmHg for >30 min despite fluids; lactate >4 mmol/L; NEWS2 ≥7; need for vasopressors |
| **VT / VF** | Immediately | Cardiac arrest team, senior | Pulseless → defibrillate. Stable VT with pulse → call senior, consider amiodarone |
| **High-degree AV block** (3rd degree, 2:1, Mobitz II) | Urgent | Senior, cardiology | HR <40 bpm, hypotension; consider temporary pacing (transvenous or external) |
| **Mechanical complication** (new murmur, sudden hypotension) | Emergency | Senior, cardiothoracic surgery | Echo shows VSD, papillary muscle rupture, free wall rupture |
| **Failed thrombolysis** (<50% ST resolution at 60-90 min) | Urgent | Senior, cardiology (rescue PCI) | Persistent ST-elevation, ongoing pain |
| **Acute heart failure / pulmonary oedema** | Urgent | Medical registrar / cardiology | SpO2 <90% despite O2, respiratory rate >25, use NIV (CPAP/BiPAP); if fails → ICU |
| **Massive GI bleed on anti-thrombotics** | Emergency | Senior, gastroenterology, haematology | Hb drop >2 g/dL, haemodynamic instability; stop DAPT/anticoagulation temporarily (discuss risk of stent thrombosis) |
| **NEWS2 ≥7** | Immediately | Outreach team / senior doctor | Any single parameter >3 or composite >7 |

**General rule:** If you are worried about the patient, call your senior. Do not wait for the patient to deteriorate. In STEMI, early senior input (cardiologist, intensivist) improves survival.

# Acute Coronary Syndrome — STEMI

**One-liner:** Occlusive coronary thrombosis causing transmural myocardial necrosis; presents with crushing chest pain, ST-elevation on ECG, and high mortality if not reperfused within 120 minutes.

---

## 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

You are bleeped to the ED resus bay. A 58-year-old man with a 30-pack-year smoking history, known hypertension, presents with a 90-minute history of central, “elephant-on-chest” chest pain radiating to his left arm and jaw. He is pale, sweaty, and clammy. He tells you he feels “impending doom.” His heart rate is 110 bpm, BP 145/90, respiratory rate 22, oxygen saturations 94% on room air. He has had one episode of vomiting.

**Pattern Clues Dissected:**
- **Central crushing pain radiating to left arm/jaw** → classic myocardial ischaemia; referred pain via shared spinal afferents (C8-T4)
- **Pale, sweaty, clammy (diaphoresis)** → sympathetic activation from pain, anxiety, and reduced cardiac output; endogenous catecholamine surge
- **Impending doom** → autonomic storm; often a prodrome of acute MI
- **Tachycardia** → compensatory response (or pain/stress), but can be due to reduced LV function
- **Vomiting** → vagal stimulation (inferior wall MI more commonly causes nausea/vomiting due to Bezold-Jarisch reflex)
- **Smoking, hypertension** → established coronary artery disease risk factors

**Atypical Presentations (High-Yield Traps):**
1. **Silent MI in diabetics** – No chest pain. Presents with dyspnoea, fatigue, or hypotension. Autonomic neuropathy blunts afferent pain signals.
2. **Elderly patient with acute confusion** – Myocardial ischaemia can present as delirium, especially in those with baseline cognitive impairment. No chest pain complaint.
3. **Woman with epigastric pain/nausea** – Often misdiagnosed as dyspepsia. Females more likely to have non-obstructive CAD (MINOCA) but still can have ST-elevation.

---

## 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

STEMI = ST-Elevation Myocardial Infarction. It is the most severe form of acute coronary syndrome, defined by complete thrombotic occlusion of a coronary artery causing transmural ischaemia and necrosis of the myocardium supplied by that artery. The diagnosis requires both elevated cardiac biomarkers (troponin) and characteristic ECG changes (ST-elevation in two contiguous leads, or new LBBB).

**Why it matters:**
- **Frequency:** ~100,000 STEMIs per year in the UK. Leading cause of death globally.
- **Stakes:** Time = muscle. Every 30-minute delay to reperfusion increases 1-year mortality by ~7.5%. Early recognition and PCI/thrombolysis saves lives.
- **Common pitfalls:** Missing atypical presentations (especially in women/diabetics); failing to repeat ECG after 10 minutes if initial is non-diagnostic; delaying senior involvement.

---

## 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**The story of a STEMI:**

It begins with **vulnerable plaque rupture** or erosion in a coronary artery. The plaque has a thin fibrous cap, a large lipid-rich core, and inflammatory cells (macrophages, T-lymphocytes). When the cap ruptures, subendothelial tissue factor is exposed to blood, triggering the **coagulation cascade**. Platelets adhere, activate, and aggregate. The fibrin mesh forms a **white clot** that grows into an **occlusive red thrombus** (fibrin-rich). This complete occlusion halts blood flow distal to the lesion.

- **Within 20 minutes:** Myocardial cells switch to anaerobic metabolism. ATP depleted. Sodium-potassium pump fails. Cell swelling.

**Mechanism -> Key Clinical Signs:**

| Pathophysiological Step | Clinical Manifestation |
|------------------------|------------------------|
| Complete coronary occlusion | ST-elevation on ECG (due to transmural ischaemia causing current of injury); reciprocal ST-depression in opposite leads |
| Myocyte necrosis | Troponin release (cTnI/cTnT); also CK-MB (less specific, faster rise) |
| Reduced LV systolic function | Hypotension, pulmonary oedema, cardiogenic shock if extensive |
| Sympathetic activation | Diaphoresis, pallor, tachycardia |
| Vagal stimulation (especially inferior MI, right coronary artery) | Nausea, vomiting, bradycardia, hypotension (Bezold-Jarisch reflex) |
| Ischaemic pain | Central chest pain radiating to arms/jaw due to referred pain via cardiac afferents (C8-T4) |
| Electrical instability | Ventricular arrhythmias (VT, VF) due to ischaemic myocardium acting as focus |

**Connect back to pattern recognition:** The patient's diaphoresis and tachycardia = sympathetic surge + reduced stroke volume. The vomiting = vagal activation from inferior ischaemia (often RCA occlusion). The ST-elevation on ECG = transmural current of injury.

## 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

**Most common cause:** Atherosclerotic plaque rupture (>95% of STEMIs).

| Category | Specific Cause | Stem Clue to Suspect |
|----------|---------------|----------------------|
| **Atherosclerotic** | Spontaneous plaque rupture with occlusive thrombus | Classic risk factors: smoking, HTN, DM, dyslipidaemia, age >45 men, >55 women |
| **Embolic** | Coronary artery embolism (e.g., from atrial fibrillation, infective endocarditis, prosthetic valve) | Sudden onset, no prodromal angina, known AF or recent valve surgery; may affect multiple territories |
| **Spontaneous coronary artery dissection (SCAD)** | Intimal tear leading to intramural haematoma (no atherosclerosis) | Young female, peripartum, fibromuscular dysplasia, intense physical/emotional stress |
| **Coronary vasospasm (variant/Prinzmetal angina)** | Focal epicardial coronary spasm (may occur at rest, often nocturnal) | Transient ST-elevation (often in sleep), associated with smoking/cocaine/methamphetamine; responds to vasodilators |
| **Thrombophilic states** | Hypercoagulable conditions (e.g., antiphospholipid syndrome, factor V Leiden, thrombocytosis) | History of prior unprovoked VTE; positive lupus anticoagulant; young age without traditional risk factors |
| **Cocaine/amphetamine use** | Vasospasm + increased O2 demand + prothrombotic state | Young patient, acute presentation, history of recreational drug use; often with agitation, tachycardia, hypertension |
| **Myocardial infarction with non-obstructive coronary arteries (MINOCA)** | No culprit stenosis >50%: can be due to coronary spasm, microvascular dysfunction, embolism, or myocarditis mimic | Females more common; angiography shows mild/no disease; requires further evaluation (MRI, OCT) |

**Key point:** Always consider SCAD in young women (especially peripartum) – do not give standard anti-thrombotic therapy blindly; discuss with cardiology.

---

## 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria (Fourth Universal Definition of MI, 2018):**  
- Rise/fall of cardiac troponin with at least one value >99th percentile upper reference limit, **AND**  
- At least one of:  
  - Symptoms of myocardial ischaemia  
  - New ischaemic ECG changes (ST-T changes, LBBB)  
  - Development of pathological Q waves  
  - Imaging evidence of new loss of viable myocardium or regional wall motion abnormality  
  - Identification of coronary thrombus on angiography  

**For STEMI specifically:** ST-elevation ≥1 mm in two contiguous limb leads (or ≥2 mm in two contiguous chest leads) **plus** positive troponin. But **do not wait for troponin** before activating the cath lab – ECG is the gatekeeper.

| Test | What It Shows | Why Order It | How to Interpret | Pattern Recognition |
|------|---------------|--------------|------------------|---------------------|
| **12-lead ECG** | ST-elevation, reciprocal depression, Q waves, new LBBB | Diagnostic for STEMI; localises culprit artery; identifies high-risk features (e.g., anterior ST-elevation, widespread hypotension) | *Isolated finding that confirms STEMI:* ST-elevation ≥1mm in contiguous leads (except V2-V3: ≥2mm in men ≥40y, ≥1.5mm in women) | Anterior leads V1-V4: LAD occlusion. Inferior leads II, III, aVF: RCA (often with reciprocal in aVL) or LCx. Posterior involvement: ST-depression V1-V3 ± tall R wave. |
| **High-sensitivity troponin (hs-cTnI or hs-cTnT)** | Cardiac myocyte necrosis markers | Confirms myocardial injury; prognostic value; guides timing of repeat measurement | **Single elevated value >99th percentile + dynamic change (rise/fall) = acute MI.** In STEMI, often very high (>10x ULN). | In STEMI, troponin may be normal initially if very early (<2h from pain onset); repeat at 3-6h. A single normal doesn't rule out. |
| **Chest X-ray** | Heart size, pulmonary oedema, aortic contour | Rule out other causes (dissection, pneumothorax); assess for heart failure | Cardiomegaly suggests chronic HTN; pulmonary oedema = acute LV failure; widened mediastinum → consider aortic dissection | Do not delay reperfusion for CXR if STEMI is clear |
| **Echocardiogram** | Regional wall motion abnormalities, LVEF, mechanical complications | Assess extent of ischaemia; detect complications (VSD, papillary muscle rupture, pericardial effusion) | New wall motion abnormality supports diagnosis; LVEF <40% indicates high risk; pericardial effusion ± tamponade suggests complications | Not essential for diagnosis in STEMI if clear ECG – may be done post-PCI |
| **Bloods (FBC, U&E, glucose, lipids)** | Baseline kidney function, electrolytes, anaemia, infection | Guided therapy (renal function for anti-thrombotics); correct hypokalaemia/hypomagnesaemia to reduce arrhythmia risk | Potassium <4.0 → high risk of VT/VF. eGFR <30 → cautious with LMWH/GPIIb/IIIa. | Always check glucose: stress hyperglycaemia worsens outcomes |

**What single finding confirms / rules out?**  
- **Confirms STEMI:** New ST-elevation in ≥2 contiguous leads + rise/fall in troponin.  
- **Rules out STEMI:** Normal ECG (no ST-elevation) and normal troponin (though unstable angina/NSTEMI possible – different topic).

---

## 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate — First Hour (Door-to-Balloon / Door-to-Needle target: ≤120 minutes from diagnosis)**

1. **MONA** (no longer standard – only Oxygen if hypoxic)  
   - **Morphine** 2-5 mg IV (titrate) → reduces pain, anxiety, and preload; caution: respiratory depression, nausea.  
   - **Oxygen** if SpO2 <90% → improves oxygenation; **do not give blindly** – hyperoxia can cause vasoconstriction and worsen injury.  
   - **Nitrates** GTN 400mcg sublingual (SL) every 5 min up to 3 doses → vasodilates coronary arteries and reduces preload; caution in RV infarct/hypotension.  
   - **Aspirin** 300 mg PO (chewed) → immediate platelet cyclooxygenase inhibition; reduces mortality by 23%.  

2. **Dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT)**  
   - **Prasugrel** 60 mg PO loading dose once (preferred over clopidogrel in STEMI) → faster, more potent P2Y12 inhibition.  
   - *Or* **Ticagrelor** 180 mg PO loading dose if prasugrel contraindicated (e.g., prior stroke/TIA, weight <60kg, age >75 for prasugrel caution).  
   - *Or* **Clopidogrel** 300-600 mg PO if above unavailable (less potent, slower onset).  

3. **Anticoagulation**  
   - **Unfractionated heparin (UFH):** 70-100 IU/kg IV bolus (max 5000 IU) → bolus then infusion (target APTT 50-70s). Used pre-PCI.  
   - *Or* **Enoxaparin** 0.5 mg/kg IV (then SC BID) – increasingly preferred in some protocols; check eGFR.  

4. **Reperfusion strategy**  
   - **Primary PCI (pPCI)** – if within 12 hours of symptom onset AND door-to-balloon <120 min AND PCI-capable hospital. Gold standard.  
     - Procedure: balloon angioplasty of culprit lesion + stent.  
     - While waiting: give UFH/enoxaparin, DAPT as above.  
   - **Thrombolysis** (e.g., alteplase, tenecteplase) – if pPCI not available within 120 min.  
     - Give within 30 min of diagnosis (door-to-needle).  
     - Contraindications: recent stroke, active bleeding, severe hypertension, aortic dissection.  
   - **Rescue PCI** – if thrombolysis fails (persistent ST-elevation >50% at 60-90 min, persistent pain).  

5. **Beta-blocker**  
   - **Bisoprolol (or metoprolol) PO** (e.g., bisoprolol 2.5-5 mg od) – reduce myocardial O2 demand, prevent arrhythmias. Contraindicated if acute heart failure, bradycardia, hypotension, cardiogenic shock.  

6. **High-dose statin**  
   - **Atorvastatin 80 mg PO** once daily – plaque stabilisation, lipid lowering, pleiotropic effects (anti-inflammatory). Start ASAP regardless of baseline LDL.  

**What NOT to do (common pitfalls):**  
- Do not give IV beta-blocker in acute phase (risk of cardiogenic shock) – use oral only.  
- Do not give nitrates if RV infarct (hypotension).  
- Do not delay PCI to wait for troponin result.  
- Do not give thrombolysis if patient has had a stroke in last 6 months or active bleeding.  

**Ward Management — 24-72 hours**

- Continue **DAPT** (aspirin 75 mg od lifelong + prasugrel 10 mg od / ticagrelor 90 mg bd for 12 months).  
- **Anticoagulation** (e.g., enoxaparin SC BID or UFH infusion) for at least 48 hours post-thrombolysis or until PCI.  
- **Beta-blocker** titrated to resting HR 50-60 bpm.  
- **ACE inhibitor** (e.g., ramipril 2.5-5 mg od) – start within 24h if BP permits, especially if LVEF <40%, anterior MI, or heart failure. Reduces remodelling and mortality.  
- **Mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist** (e.g., spironolactone 12.5-25 mg od) if LVEF ≤40% and symptomatic heart failure or diabetes (eplerenone if gynaecomastia).  
- **Monitor for complications** – arrhythmias, heart failure, bleeding, reinfarction.  
- **Echocardiogram** to assess LV function before discharge.  

**Long-Term Management**

- **Lifestyle:** smoking cessation, Mediterranean diet, exercise (cardiac rehabilitation), weight loss.  
- **Medical therapy:**  
  - Aspirin 75 mg od lifelong  
  - P2Y12 inhibitor for 12 months (prasugrel/ticagrelor) then switch to clopidogrel or aspirin alone  
  - High-dose statin (atorvastatin 80 mg od) lifelong  
  - Beta-blocker (e.g., bisoprolol 5-10 mg od) lifelong if MI within 3 years or LV dysfunction  
  - ACE inhibitor (e.g., ramipril 5-10 mg od) lifelong if LVEF <40% or hypertension  
- **Dual pathway inhibition** (rivaroxaban 2.5 mg bd + aspirin) if high-risk (e.g., polyvascular disease) and low bleeding risk – discuss with cardiology.  
- **Implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD)** if LVEF ≤35% despite ≥40 days of optimal medical therapy.  

---

## 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | STEMI | NSTEMI | Aortic Dissection | Acute Pericarditis |
|---------|-------|--------|-------------------|--------------------|
| **Pain** | Central crushing, radiating to arm/jaw, ± nausea, lasts >30 min | Similar but may be less severe, intermittent, or atypical | Sudden, severe, tearing anterior chest pain radiating to back, interscapular; may migrate | Sharp, pleuritic pain, worse lying flat, better leaning forward; often positional |
| **Exam** | Diaphoresis, pallor, ± hypotension, ± signs of heart failure | Similar but often less dramatic | Pulse deficit, BP difference >20 mmHg between arms, aortic regurgitation murmur, neurological deficits | Pericardial rub (triphasic), ± low-grade fever |
| **ECG** | ST-elevation in contiguous leads, reciprocal depression, new Q waves | ST-depression ± T wave inversion; no sustained ST-elevation (transient possible) | No specific pattern; may have ST-elevation if coronary ostia involved (rare); LVH strain pattern may mimic | Diffuse ST-elevation (concave upward) without reciprocal depression; PR depression |
| **Labs** | Troponin elevated (high), dynamic change; CK-MB may be high | Troponin elevated (any) but may be lower | Troponin normal or mildly elevated if coronary involved; D-dimer very high (rule out, not rule in) | Troponin normal or mildly elevated (<2x ULN); inflammatory markers (CRP, ESR) high |
| **Key diff** | Coronary angiography shows occlusive thrombus | Angiography shows non-occlusive stenosis or plaque ulceration | CT aortogram with contrast shows intimal flap and false lumen | Echocardiogram shows pericardial effusion, no wall motion abnormality; clinical response to NSAIDs |

---

## 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

| Complication | Presentation | Key Signs | What to Do |
|--------------|--------------|-----------|------------|
| **Cardiogenic shock** | Persistent hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg) despite fluids, end-organ hypoperfusion, altered mental status, oliguria | Cold clammy skin, tachycardia, narrow pulse pressure, raised JVP, pulmonary oedema | Call senior, ICU, cardiac catheterisation; urgent revascularisation (IABP/impella supportive); do not give beta-blockers |
| **Ventricular tachyarrhythmia (VT/VF)** | Sudden collapse, pulseless, cardiac arrest | ECG: VT/VF | Defibrillate (200J biphasic), CPR, amiodarone 300 mg IV after 3 shocks; treat underlying ischaemia |
| **Acute LV failure / pulmonary oedema** | Severe dyspnoea, orthopnoea, pink frothy sputum | Crackles throughout lung fields, raised JVP, SpO2 ↓ | Sit patient up, O2, furosemide 40-80 mg IV, nitrates if BP allows, CPAP, consider urgent PCI |
| **Right ventricular infarction** | Hypotension, clear lungs, raised JVP, Kussmaul's sign (JVP rises on inspiration) | ST-elevation in V4R (right-sided ECG), inferior MI | Fluid bolus (500-1000 mL crystalloid), do NOT give nitrates or diuretics; urgent revascularisation |
| **Mechanical complications** (free wall rupture, VSD, papillary muscle rupture) | Sudden severe hypotension, refractory heart failure, new pansystolic murmur (VSD/mitral regurgitation), syncope | ~3-7 days post-MI. Free wall rupture: electromechanical dissociation → cardiac arrest. VSD: harsh murmur at LLSB. Papillary muscle: acute mitral regurgitation, pulmonary oedema | Emergency echo, call cardiothoracic surgery; treat underlying (surgery/transcatheter repair) |
| **Pericarditis / Dressler syndrome** | Pleuritic chest pain, fever, pericardial rub 2-6 weeks post MI | Diffuse ST-elevation, PR depression, pericardial effusion on echo | NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400-800 mg TID) + colchicine; avoid steroids if possible (increase rupture risk) |
| **Late-presentation patterns** (untreated) | If missed and no reperfusion: patient presents with established Q waves on ECG, LV dysfunction, chronic heart failure, ventricular aneurysm, mural thrombus ± embolism | Q waves, low voltage, dyskinetic segment on echo | Anticoagulation if LV thrombus; ACEi, beta-blocker, diuretic for heart failure; ICD if LVEF ≤35% |

---

## 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

1. **STEMI Differential — "ABCDE"**  
   - A – Aortic dissection  
   - B – Boerhaave syndrome (oesophageal rupture) – pain + vomiting  
   - C – Cardiac (MI, pericarditis)  
   - D – Diaphragmatic (pneumothorax, PE)  
   - E – Epigastric (pancreatitis, biliary colic)  

2. **Indications for Thrombolysis in STEMI — "TIMI"** (not a strong mnemonic, but recall: time <12h, ST-elevation >1mm, new LBBB, ongoing pain) – but better:  
   **"Time Is Muscle"** – symptom onset <12h; contraindications: **"I BLEED"**  
   - I – Intracranial bleed ever  
   - B – Bleeding disorder / active bleed  
   - L – Liver disease severe  
   - E – Eye surgery (<6 weeks)  
   - E – Epilepsy (stroke mimic? ensure not SAH)  
   - D – Dissection (aortic)  

3. **ECG localisation of culprit artery — "LAD RCA LCx"**  
   - **LAD** (anterior/septal leads V1-4): anterior MI, worse prognosis  
   - **RCA** (inferior leads II, III, aVF; ± RV leads V4R): often bradycardia, hypotension (Bezold-Jarisch)  
   - **LCx** (lateral leads I, aVL, V5-6): often smaller area; can cause posterior MI (ST-depression V1-3)

**5-7 Clinical Pearls (Pattern Recognition Cues):**

1. **"Reciprocal ST-depression is the hallmark of STEMI"** – if you see ST-elevation in inferior leads, check aVL for reciprocal depression (rules out pericarditis).  
2. **"New LBBB + pain = STEMI until proven otherwise"** – activate cath lab immediately. Consider Sgarbossa criteria for diagnosis.  
3. **"In MI, troponin rises, but ECG is the gatekeeper"** – don't wait for troponin to make the call.  
4. **"Inferior MI with hypotension + clear lungs = RV infarct"** – give fluids, not nitrates.  
5. **"Posterior MI mimics anterior ST-depression"** – look for tall R waves in V1-3 and ST-elevation in posterior leads (V7-V9).  
6. **"Women and diabetics present atypically (dyspnoea, fatigue)"** – always do an ECG in at-risk patients with unexplained dyspnoea.  
7. **"Time from symptom onset to reperfusion is the single strongest predictor of outcome"** – target <120 min for PCI, <30 min for thrombolysis.

**Common Mistakes:**

- **Giving oxygen when SpO2 >95%** – harms (hyperoxia worsens ischaemia).  
- **Delaying PCI to wait for troponin** – ECG is sufficient.  
- **Giving beta-blocker in acute heart failure or cardiogenic shock** – can precipitate death.  
- **Assuming normal troponin + pain = no MI** – unstable angina exists (troponin negative).  
- **Forgetting to give a statin** – reduces recurrent events and mortality.

---

## 10. Escalation Criteria

| Condition | When to Call | Who to Call | Specific Thresholds |
|-----------|--------------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Cardiogenic shock** | Immediately | Senior (Reg/Cons), ICU, Cardiac Catheter Lab | SBP <90 mmHg for >30 min despite fluids; lactate >4 mmol/L; NEWS2 ≥7; need for vasopressors |
| **VT / VF** | Immediately | Cardiac arrest team, senior | Pulseless → defibrillate. Stable VT with pulse → call senior, consider amiodarone |
| **High-degree AV block** (3rd degree, 2:1, Mobitz II) | Urgent | Senior, cardiology | HR <40 bpm, hypotension; consider temporary pacing (transvenous or external) |
| **Mechanical complication** (new murmur, sudden hypotension) | Emergency | Senior, cardiothoracic surgery | Echo shows VSD, papillary muscle rupture, free wall rupture |
| **Failed thrombolysis** (<50% ST resolution at 60-90 min) | Urgent | Senior, cardiology (rescue PCI) | Persistent ST-elevation, ongoing pain |
| **Acute heart failure / pulmonary oedema** | Urgent | Medical registrar / cardiology | SpO2 <90% despite O2, respiratory rate >25, use NIV (CPAP/BiPAP); if fails → ICU |
| **Massive GI bleed on anti-thrombotics** | Emergency | Senior, gastroenterology, haematology | Hb drop >2 g/dL, haemodynamic instability; stop DAPT/anticoagulation temporarily (discuss risk of stent thrombosis) |
| **NEWS2 ≥7** | Immediately | Outreach team / senior doctor | Any single parameter >3 or composite >7 |

**General rule:** If you are worried about the patient, call your senior. Do not wait for the patient to deteriorate. In STEMI, early senior input (cardiologist, intensivist) improves survival.
